link|Q54153260}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Takachiniimasu Shrine |Image = [[File:Takachiniimasu-jinja, shaden.jpg|280px]]
Main sanctuary |Location = {{jalink|Kōchi Prefecture|Q134093}}{{jalink|Sukumo City|Q655258}} Hirata-chō Tonai |Coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deity = {{jalink|Kotoshironushi|Q8009611|Tomihayayae Kotoshironushi-no-Kami}} |Sacred object = |Shrine rank = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (minor)
Former {{jalink|Gōsha|Q134917316}} |Founded = Unknown |Annual festival = {{jalink|September 22 (old calendar)|Q840122|Old calendar September 22}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}} |Main hall = Three-bay {{jalink|Nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} |Pilgrimage designation = |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Kochi|label=Takachiniimasu Shrine|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[File:Takachiniimasu-jinja, torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Takachiniimasu Shrine''' (たかちにますじんじゃ / たかちにいますじんじゃ, also written Takachiza Shrine) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} located in Hirata-chō Tonai, {{jalink|Sukumo City|Q655258}}, {{jalink|Kōchi Prefecture|Q134093}}. It is a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}, and under the {{jalink|Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|former ranking system}} it was classified as a {{jalink|Gōsha|Q134917316}}. === Enshrined deities === The shrine enshrines the following three deities{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}}. * Principal deity: {{jalink|Kotoshironushi|Q8009611|Tomihayayae Kotoshironushi-no-Kami}} * Right auxiliary shrine: {{jalink|Ōkuninushi|Q276944|Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto}} * Left auxiliary shrine: {{jalink|Susanoo|Q272993|Susanoo-no-Mikoto}} In the ''{{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}}'' {{jalink|Jinmyōchō|Q11064932}} compiled in {{jalink|Engi (era)|Q1327274}} 5 ({{jalink|927|Q30470}}), it is listed simply as “Takachiniimasu Shrine (the shrine of the deity enshrined at Takachi),” without specifying the enshrined deities. The name “{{Ruby|Takachi|たかち}}” is an ancient toponym unrelated to the present-day place name of Kōchi City (which developed during the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}} through the changes Kawauchi → Taka-chi → Kōchi“Kōchi Castle,” ''Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 40: Place Names of Kōchi Prefecture'', Heibonsha, 1983.). In historical sources it also appears as “Takaji” or “Takamochi”{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. Its origin is unclear, but ''Tosa no Kuni Shikishakō'' points out a phonetic correspondence between “Takachi” and “Takaichi” (in {{jalink|Yamato Province|Q907495}} {{jalink|Takaichi District|Q1117901}} there is {{jalink|Kawamata Shrine|Q11553360|Kamo Kotoshironushi Shrine}}){{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. According to ''Tosa no Kuni Shikishakō'', the {{jalink|shintai|Q327532}} of the principal deity is a blue-black jade stone; that of the left auxiliary deity is a blue stone; and that of the right auxiliary deity is a wooden statue{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}}. === History === The date of founding is unknown{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. Nearby stood one of Kōchi Prefecture’s representative tumuli, the {{jalink|Hirata Sogayama Kofun|Q22120134}} (now lost), and a theory has been proposed that this kofun and the shrine together formed the tomb and ritual site of the {{jalink|Hata no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Q21654186}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. The ''{{jalink|Sendai Kuji Hongi|Q2716751}}'' “{{jalink|Kokuzō Hongi|Q133833194}}” records that during the reign of the 10th emperor {{jalink|Emperor Sujin|Q333530}}, Amenokara-sumi-no-Mikoto was appointed Hata no Kuni no Miyatsuko, leading to a theory that the shrine was founded around this time, though details are unclear{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}}. Ritual remains have been identified within the precincts, and artifacts such as {{jalink|Haji ware|Q5639938}}, {{jalink|Sue ware|Q7634351}}, and jars from the {{jalink|Heian period|Q193292}} have been excavated, as well as a chipped stone axe from the late {{jalink|Jōmon period|Q52824}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}}. In the ''{{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}}'' {{jalink|Jinmyōchō|Q11064932}} compiled in {{jalink|Engi (era)|Q1327274}} 5 ({{jalink|927|Q30470}}), the shrine is listed in {{jalink|Tosa Province|Q857330}} {{jalink|Hata District|Q1132241}} as “Takachiniimasu Shrine” and ranked as a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}}. Among the place names appearing in the ''Wamyōshō'', the present location is identified with Hirata-gō (also written Makita-gō in variant texts) of Hata District{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}}. During the {{jalink|medieval period|Q12554}}, there was a repair of the shrine buildings in {{jalink|Genkyū|Q1194276}} 2 ({{jalink|1205|Q5229}}), and Chōfuku-ji served as its {{jalink|bettō-ji|Q11396755}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}}. An inscription on a waniguchi bell dated {{jalink|Ōei|Q1193078}} 6 ({{jalink|1399|Q6422}}) cited in ''Nanroshi'' reads “Takachi no Daimyōjin,” while a ridge tag from {{jalink|Tenbun (era)|Q1192853}} 13 ({{jalink|1544|Q6525}}) reads “Takamochisha Daimyōjin,” recording construction carried out by {{jalink|Ichijō Fusayori|Q11352818}}, the fourth head of the {{jalink|Tosa Ichijō clan|Q11423000}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}}. A land survey register from {{jalink|Tenshō|Q1145058}} 17 ({{jalink|1589|Q6759}}) refers to “Takamochi Shrine” and records the existence of a shrine floor, treasure hall, dance hall, and side hall{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}}. In the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}}, the shrine was venerated by the Yamauchi (Andō) family, chief retainers of the {{jalink|Tosa Domain|Q1189704}} at Sukumo Doi, and in {{jalink|Meiwa|Q1146161}} 5 ({{jalink|1768|Q7675}}) the present main hall was rebuilt by the seventh-generation retainer {{jalink|Yamauchi Ujitsura|Q11466439}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. After the {{jalink|Meiji Restoration|Q8707}}, the shrine was ranked as a {{jalink|Gōsha|Q134917316}} under the {{jalink|Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}}. === Shrine grounds === [[File:Takachiniimasu-jinja, honden.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Main hall (Kōchi Prefecture–designated cultural property), protective enclosure}}]] The main hall was rebuilt in {{jalink|Meiwa|Q1146161}} 5 ({{jalink|1768|Q7675}}) during the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}} by the seventh-generation Sukumo Doi chief retainer {{jalink|Yamauchi Ujitsura|Q11466439}} and his heir Ujimasa. It is a three-bay {{jalink|Nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} structure with a cypress-bark roof. The interior is divided into an inner and outer sanctuary. Some carvings—such as elephant-nose brackets, frog-leg struts, and decorative supports—date from an early Edo reconstruction. The building is designated a Protected Tangible Cultural Property of Kōchi Prefecture and is currently enshrined within a protective enclosure{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}{{Sfn|Cultural Properties of Sukumo City (Sukumo City History)}}. The {{jalink|Ichiigashi|Q3414213}} (Castanopsis cuspidata) in front of the shrine is the largest tree in the shrine grove, measuring 29.0 meters in height and estimated to be 400 years old. It is designated a Natural Monument of Sukumo City{{Sfn|Cultural Properties of Sukumo City (Sukumo City History)}}On-site explanatory sign.. File:Takachiniimasu-jinja, ichiigashi.jpg|Ichiigashi (Sukumo City–designated Natural Monument) === Auxiliary and branch shrines === [[File:Takachiniimasu-jinja, keidaisha-2.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Left shrine}}]] [[File:Takachiniimasu-jinja, keidaisha-1.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Right shrine}}]] * Left shrine (enshrined to the left when facing the main hall) ** Hijiri Shrine ** Kōtaishi Shrine ** Soga Shrine ** Tenmangū * Right shrine (enshrined to the right when facing the main hall) ** Shibaori Shrine ** Niida Shrine ** Ōfune Shrine ** Hiryū Shrine ** Taka Shrine === Cultural properties === === Kōchi Prefecture–designated cultural properties === * Protected Tangible Cultural Property ** Main hall (structure) – Edo period; rebuilt in Meiwa 5 (1768). Designated on February 15, 1955 (Shōwa 30)[http://www.kochinet.ed.jp/bunkazai/details/110-2/110-2-07.htm Takachiza Shrine Main Hall] (Kōchi Prefecture website).[http://www.city.sukumo.kochi.jp/sbc/history/cultural/p002.html Takachiniimasu Shrine Main Hall] (Sukumo City website).{{Sfn|Cultural Properties of Sukumo City (Sukumo City History)}}. === Sukumo City–designated cultural properties === * Protected Tangible Cultural Property ** Half-bodied lion statues – Works from the {{jalink|Kamakura period|Q236205}}. Two Karajishi statues made of {{jalink|hinoki|Q917187}} wood, each 32.5 cm tall; both lack half of the body. Designated on July 24, 1963 (Shōwa 38){{Sfn|Cultural Properties of Sukumo City (Sukumo City History)}}. * Natural Monument ** Hirata no Ichiigashi – Designated on July 24, 1963 (Shōwa 38){{Sfn|Cultural Properties of Sukumo City (Sukumo City History)}}. === Notes === {{Help:Footnotes}} {{Reflist|2}} === References === * On-site explanatory sign * Publications by local governments ** [http://www.city.sukumo.kochi.jp/sbc/history/sisi/ ''Sukumo City History''] Sukumo City. – Link to the Sukumo City Sukumo History Museum. *** {{Wikicite|reference=[http://www.city.sukumo.kochi.jp/sbc/history/sisi/132901.html “Cultural Properties of Sukumo City”]|ref={{Harvid|Cultural Properties of Sukumo City (Sukumo City History)}}}}. * Main sanctuary |Location = {{jalink|Kōchi Prefecture|Q134093}}{{jalink|Sukumo City|Q655258}} Hirata-chō Tonai |Coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deity = {{jalink|Kotoshironushi|Q8009611|Tomihayayae Kotoshironushi-no-Kami}} |Sacred object = |Shrine rank = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (minor)
Former {{jalink|Gōsha|Q134917316}} |Founded = Unknown |Annual festival = {{jalink|September 22 (old calendar)|Q840122|Old calendar September 22}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}} |Main hall = Three-bay {{jalink|Nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} |Pilgrimage designation = |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Kochi|label=Takachiniimasu Shrine|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[File:Takachiniimasu-jinja, torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Takachiniimasu Shrine''' (たかちにますじんじゃ / たかちにいますじんじゃ, also written Takachiza Shrine) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} located in Hirata-chō Tonai, {{jalink|Sukumo City|Q655258}}, {{jalink|Kōchi Prefecture|Q134093}}. It is a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}, and under the {{jalink|Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|former ranking system}} it was classified as a {{jalink|Gōsha|Q134917316}}. === Enshrined deities === The shrine enshrines the following three deities{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}}. * Principal deity: {{jalink|Kotoshironushi|Q8009611|Tomihayayae Kotoshironushi-no-Kami}} * Right auxiliary shrine: {{jalink|Ōkuninushi|Q276944|Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto}} * Left auxiliary shrine: {{jalink|Susanoo|Q272993|Susanoo-no-Mikoto}} In the ''{{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}}'' {{jalink|Jinmyōchō|Q11064932}} compiled in {{jalink|Engi (era)|Q1327274}} 5 ({{jalink|927|Q30470}}), it is listed simply as “Takachiniimasu Shrine (the shrine of the deity enshrined at Takachi),” without specifying the enshrined deities. The name “{{Ruby|Takachi|たかち}}” is an ancient toponym unrelated to the present-day place name of Kōchi City (which developed during the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}} through the changes Kawauchi → Taka-chi → Kōchi“Kōchi Castle,” ''Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 40: Place Names of Kōchi Prefecture'', Heibonsha, 1983.). In historical sources it also appears as “Takaji” or “Takamochi”{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. Its origin is unclear, but ''Tosa no Kuni Shikishakō'' points out a phonetic correspondence between “Takachi” and “Takaichi” (in {{jalink|Yamato Province|Q907495}} {{jalink|Takaichi District|Q1117901}} there is {{jalink|Kawamata Shrine|Q11553360|Kamo Kotoshironushi Shrine}}){{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. According to ''Tosa no Kuni Shikishakō'', the {{jalink|shintai|Q327532}} of the principal deity is a blue-black jade stone; that of the left auxiliary deity is a blue stone; and that of the right auxiliary deity is a wooden statue{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}}. === History === The date of founding is unknown{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. Nearby stood one of Kōchi Prefecture’s representative tumuli, the {{jalink|Hirata Sogayama Kofun|Q22120134}} (now lost), and a theory has been proposed that this kofun and the shrine together formed the tomb and ritual site of the {{jalink|Hata no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Q21654186}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. The ''{{jalink|Sendai Kuji Hongi|Q2716751}}'' “{{jalink|Kokuzō Hongi|Q133833194}}” records that during the reign of the 10th emperor {{jalink|Emperor Sujin|Q333530}}, Amenokara-sumi-no-Mikoto was appointed Hata no Kuni no Miyatsuko, leading to a theory that the shrine was founded around this time, though details are unclear{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}}. Ritual remains have been identified within the precincts, and artifacts such as {{jalink|Haji ware|Q5639938}}, {{jalink|Sue ware|Q7634351}}, and jars from the {{jalink|Heian period|Q193292}} have been excavated, as well as a chipped stone axe from the late {{jalink|Jōmon period|Q52824}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}}. In the ''{{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}}'' {{jalink|Jinmyōchō|Q11064932}} compiled in {{jalink|Engi (era)|Q1327274}} 5 ({{jalink|927|Q30470}}), the shrine is listed in {{jalink|Tosa Province|Q857330}} {{jalink|Hata District|Q1132241}} as “Takachiniimasu Shrine” and ranked as a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}}. Among the place names appearing in the ''Wamyōshō'', the present location is identified with Hirata-gō (also written Makita-gō in variant texts) of Hata District{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}}. During the {{jalink|medieval period|Q12554}}, there was a repair of the shrine buildings in {{jalink|Genkyū|Q1194276}} 2 ({{jalink|1205|Q5229}}), and Chōfuku-ji served as its {{jalink|bettō-ji|Q11396755}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}}. An inscription on a waniguchi bell dated {{jalink|Ōei|Q1193078}} 6 ({{jalink|1399|Q6422}}) cited in ''Nanroshi'' reads “Takachi no Daimyōjin,” while a ridge tag from {{jalink|Tenbun (era)|Q1192853}} 13 ({{jalink|1544|Q6525}}) reads “Takamochisha Daimyōjin,” recording construction carried out by {{jalink|Ichijō Fusayori|Q11352818}}, the fourth head of the {{jalink|Tosa Ichijō clan|Q11423000}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}}. A land survey register from {{jalink|Tenshō|Q1145058}} 17 ({{jalink|1589|Q6759}}) refers to “Takamochi Shrine” and records the existence of a shrine floor, treasure hall, dance hall, and side hall{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}}. In the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}}, the shrine was venerated by the Yamauchi (Andō) family, chief retainers of the {{jalink|Tosa Domain|Q1189704}} at Sukumo Doi, and in {{jalink|Meiwa|Q1146161}} 5 ({{jalink|1768|Q7675}}) the present main hall was rebuilt by the seventh-generation retainer {{jalink|Yamauchi Ujitsura|Q11466439}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. After the {{jalink|Meiji Restoration|Q8707}}, the shrine was ranked as a {{jalink|Gōsha|Q134917316}} under the {{jalink|Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}}. === Shrine grounds === [[File:Takachiniimasu-jinja, honden.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Main hall (Kōchi Prefecture–designated cultural property), protective enclosure}}]] The main hall was rebuilt in {{jalink|Meiwa|Q1146161}} 5 ({{jalink|1768|Q7675}}) during the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}} by the seventh-generation Sukumo Doi chief retainer {{jalink|Yamauchi Ujitsura|Q11466439}} and his heir Ujimasa. It is a three-bay {{jalink|Nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} structure with a cypress-bark roof. The interior is divided into an inner and outer sanctuary. Some carvings—such as elephant-nose brackets, frog-leg struts, and decorative supports—date from an early Edo reconstruction. The building is designated a Protected Tangible Cultural Property of Kōchi Prefecture and is currently enshrined within a protective enclosure{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}{{Sfn|Cultural Properties of Sukumo City (Sukumo City History)}}. The {{jalink|Ichiigashi|Q3414213}} (Castanopsis cuspidata) in front of the shrine is the largest tree in the shrine grove, measuring 29.0 meters in height and estimated to be 400 years old. It is designated a Natural Monument of Sukumo City{{Sfn|Cultural Properties of Sukumo City (Sukumo City History)}}On-site explanatory sign.. File:Takachiniimasu-jinja, ichiigashi.jpg|Ichiigashi (Sukumo City–designated Natural Monument) === Auxiliary and branch shrines === [[File:Takachiniimasu-jinja, keidaisha-2.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Left shrine}}]] [[File:Takachiniimasu-jinja, keidaisha-1.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Right shrine}}]] * Left shrine (enshrined to the left when facing the main hall) ** Hijiri Shrine ** Kōtaishi Shrine ** Soga Shrine ** Tenmangū * Right shrine (enshrined to the right when facing the main hall) ** Shibaori Shrine ** Niida Shrine ** Ōfune Shrine ** Hiryū Shrine ** Taka Shrine === Cultural properties === === Kōchi Prefecture–designated cultural properties === * Protected Tangible Cultural Property ** Main hall (structure) – Edo period; rebuilt in Meiwa 5 (1768). Designated on February 15, 1955 (Shōwa 30)[http://www.kochinet.ed.jp/bunkazai/details/110-2/110-2-07.htm Takachiza Shrine Main Hall] (Kōchi Prefecture website).[http://www.city.sukumo.kochi.jp/sbc/history/cultural/p002.html Takachiniimasu Shrine Main Hall] (Sukumo City website).{{Sfn|Cultural Properties of Sukumo City (Sukumo City History)}}. === Sukumo City–designated cultural properties === * Protected Tangible Cultural Property ** Half-bodied lion statues – Works from the {{jalink|Kamakura period|Q236205}}. Two Karajishi statues made of {{jalink|hinoki|Q917187}} wood, each 32.5 cm tall; both lack half of the body. Designated on July 24, 1963 (Shōwa 38){{Sfn|Cultural Properties of Sukumo City (Sukumo City History)}}. * Natural Monument ** Hirata no Ichiigashi – Designated on July 24, 1963 (Shōwa 38){{Sfn|Cultural Properties of Sukumo City (Sukumo City History)}}. === Notes === {{Help:Footnotes}} {{Reflist|2}} === References === * On-site explanatory sign * Publications by local governments ** [http://www.city.sukumo.kochi.jp/sbc/history/sisi/ ''Sukumo City History''] Sukumo City. – Link to the Sukumo City Sukumo History Museum. *** It is a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}, and under the {{jalink|Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|former ranking system}} it was classified as a {{jalink|Gōsha|Q134917316}}. === Enshrined deities === The shrine enshrines the following three deities{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}}. * Principal deity: {{jalink|Kotoshironushi|Q8009611|Tomihayayae Kotoshironushi-no-Kami}} * Right auxiliary shrine: {{jalink|Ōkuninushi|Q276944|Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto}} * Left auxiliary shrine: {{jalink|Susanoo|Q272993|Susanoo-no-Mikoto}} In the ''{{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}}'' {{jalink|Jinmyōchō|Q11064932}} compiled in {{jalink|Engi (era)|Q1327274}} 5 ({{jalink|927|Q30470}}), it is listed simply as “Takachiniimasu Shrine (the shrine of the deity enshrined at Takachi),” without specifying the enshrined deities. The name “{{Ruby|Takachi|たかち}}” is an ancient toponym unrelated to the present-day place name of Kōchi City (which developed during the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}} through the changes Kawauchi → Taka-chi → Kōchi“Kōchi Castle,” ''Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 40: Place Names of Kōchi Prefecture'', Heibonsha, 1983.). In historical sources it also appears as “Takaji” or “Takamochi”{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. Its origin is unclear, but ''Tosa no Kuni Shikishakō'' points out a phonetic correspondence between “Takachi” and “Takaichi” (in {{jalink|Yamato Province|Q907495}} {{jalink|Takaichi District|Q1117901}} there is {{jalink|Kawamata Shrine|Q11553360|Kamo Kotoshironushi Shrine}}){{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. According to ''Tosa no Kuni Shikishakō'', the {{jalink|shintai|Q327532}} of the principal deity is a blue-black jade stone; that of the left auxiliary deity is a blue stone; and that of the right auxiliary deity is a wooden statue{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}}. === History === The date of founding is unknown{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. Nearby stood one of Kōchi Prefecture’s representative tumuli, the {{jalink|Hirata Sogayama Kofun|Q22120134}} (now lost), and a theory has been proposed that this kofun and the shrine together formed the tomb and ritual site of the {{jalink|Hata no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Q21654186}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. The ''{{jalink|Sendai Kuji Hongi|Q2716751}}'' “{{jalink|Kokuzō Hongi|Q133833194}}” records that during the reign of the 10th emperor {{jalink|Emperor Sujin|Q333530}}, Amenokara-sumi-no-Mikoto was appointed Hata no Kuni no Miyatsuko, leading to a theory that the shrine was founded around this time, though details are unclear{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}}. Ritual remains have been identified within the precincts, and artifacts such as {{jalink|Haji ware|Q5639938}}, {{jalink|Sue ware|Q7634351}}, and jars from the {{jalink|Heian period|Q193292}} have been excavated, as well as a chipped stone axe from the late {{jalink|Jōmon period|Q52824}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}}. In the ''{{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}}'' {{jalink|Jinmyōchō|Q11064932}} compiled in {{jalink|Engi (era)|Q1327274}} 5 ({{jalink|927|Q30470}}), the shrine is listed in {{jalink|Tosa Province|Q857330}} {{jalink|Hata District|Q1132241}} as “Takachiniimasu Shrine” and ranked as a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}}. Among the place names appearing in the ''Wamyōshō'', the present location is identified with Hirata-gō (also written Makita-gō in variant texts) of Hata District{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}}. During the {{jalink|medieval period|Q12554}}, there was a repair of the shrine buildings in {{jalink|Genkyū|Q1194276}} 2 ({{jalink|1205|Q5229}}), and Chōfuku-ji served as its {{jalink|bettō-ji|Q11396755}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}}. An inscription on a waniguchi bell dated {{jalink|Ōei|Q1193078}} 6 ({{jalink|1399|Q6422}}) cited in ''Nanroshi'' reads “Takachi no Daimyōjin,” while a ridge tag from {{jalink|Tenbun (era)|Q1192853}} 13 ({{jalink|1544|Q6525}}) reads “Takamochisha Daimyōjin,” recording construction carried out by {{jalink|Ichijō Fusayori|Q11352818}}, the fourth head of the {{jalink|Tosa Ichijō clan|Q11423000}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}}. A land survey register from {{jalink|Tenshō|Q1145058}} 17 ({{jalink|1589|Q6759}}) refers to “Takamochi Shrine” and records the existence of a shrine floor, treasure hall, dance hall, and side hall{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}}. In the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}}, the shrine was venerated by the Yamauchi (Andō) family, chief retainers of the {{jalink|Tosa Domain|Q1189704}} at Sukumo Doi, and in {{jalink|Meiwa|Q1146161}} 5 ({{jalink|1768|Q7675}}) the present main hall was rebuilt by the seventh-generation retainer {{jalink|Yamauchi Ujitsura|Q11466439}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. After the {{jalink|Meiji Restoration|Q8707}}, the shrine was ranked as a {{jalink|Gōsha|Q134917316}} under the {{jalink|Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}}. === Shrine grounds === [[File:Takachiniimasu-jinja, honden.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Main hall (Kōchi Prefecture–designated cultural property), protective enclosure}}]] The main hall was rebuilt in {{jalink|Meiwa|Q1146161}} 5 ({{jalink|1768|Q7675}}) during the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}} by the seventh-generation Sukumo Doi chief retainer {{jalink|Yamauchi Ujitsura|Q11466439}} and his heir Ujimasa. It is a three-bay {{jalink|Nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} structure with a cypress-bark roof. The interior is divided into an inner and outer sanctuary. Some carvings—such as elephant-nose brackets, frog-leg struts, and decorative supports—date from an early Edo reconstruction. The building is designated a Protected Tangible Cultural Property of Kōchi Prefecture and is currently enshrined within a protective enclosure{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}{{Sfn|Cultural Properties of Sukumo City (Sukumo City History)}}. The {{jalink|Ichiigashi|Q3414213}} (Castanopsis cuspidata) in front of the shrine is the largest tree in the shrine grove, measuring 29.0 meters in height and estimated to be 400 years old. It is designated a Natural Monument of Sukumo City{{Sfn|Cultural Properties of Sukumo City (Sukumo City History)}}On-site explanatory sign.. File:Takachiniimasu-jinja, ichiigashi.jpg|Ichiigashi (Sukumo City–designated Natural Monument) === Auxiliary and branch shrines === [[File:Takachiniimasu-jinja, keidaisha-2.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Left shrine}}]] [[File:Takachiniimasu-jinja, keidaisha-1.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Right shrine}}]] * Left shrine (enshrined to the left when facing the main hall) ** Hijiri Shrine ** Kōtaishi Shrine ** Soga Shrine ** Tenmangū * Right shrine (enshrined to the right when facing the main hall) ** Shibaori Shrine ** Niida Shrine ** Ōfune Shrine ** Hiryū Shrine ** Taka Shrine === Cultural properties === === Kōchi Prefecture–designated cultural properties === * Protected Tangible Cultural Property ** Main hall (structure) – Edo period; rebuilt in Meiwa 5 (1768). Designated on February 15, 1955 (Shōwa 30)[http://www.kochinet.ed.jp/bunkazai/details/110-2/110-2-07.htm Takachiza Shrine Main Hall] (Kōchi Prefecture website).[http://www.city.sukumo.kochi.jp/sbc/history/cultural/p002.html Takachiniimasu Shrine Main Hall] (Sukumo City website).{{Sfn|Cultural Properties of Sukumo City (Sukumo City History)}}. === Sukumo City–designated cultural properties === * Protected Tangible Cultural Property ** Half-bodied lion statues – Works from the {{jalink|Kamakura period|Q236205}}. Two Karajishi statues made of {{jalink|hinoki|Q917187}} wood, each 32.5 cm tall; both lack half of the body. Designated on July 24, 1963 (Shōwa 38){{Sfn|Cultural Properties of Sukumo City (Sukumo City History)}}. * Natural Monument ** Hirata no Ichiigashi – Designated on July 24, 1963 (Shōwa 38){{Sfn|Cultural Properties of Sukumo City (Sukumo City History)}}. === Notes === {{Help:Footnotes}} {{Reflist|2}} === References === * On-site explanatory sign * Publications by local governments ** An inscription on a waniguchi bell dated {{jalink|Ōei|Q1193078}} 6 ({{jalink|1399|Q6422}}) cited in ''Nanroshi'' reads “Takachi no Daimyōjin,” while a ridge tag from {{jalink|Tenbun (era)|Q1192853}} 13 ({{jalink|1544|Q6525}}) reads “Takamochisha Daimyōjin,” recording construction carried out by {{jalink|Ichijō Fusayori|Q11352818}}, the fourth head of the {{jalink|Tosa Ichijō clan|Q11423000}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}}. A land survey register from {{jalink|Tenshō|Q1145058}} 17 ({{jalink|1589|Q6759}}) refers to “Takamochi Shrine” and records the existence of a shrine floor, treasure hall, dance hall, and side hall{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}}. In the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}}, the shrine was venerated by the Yamauchi (Andō) family, chief retainers of the {{jalink|Tosa Domain|Q1189704}} at Sukumo Doi, and in {{jalink|Meiwa|Q1146161}} 5 ({{jalink|1768|Q7675}}) the present main hall was rebuilt by the seventh-generation retainer {{jalink|Yamauchi Ujitsura|Q11466439}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. After the {{jalink|Meiji Restoration|Q8707}}, the shrine was ranked as a {{jalink|Gōsha|Q134917316}} under the {{jalink|Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}}. === Shrine grounds === [[File:Takachiniimasu-jinja, honden.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Main hall (Kōchi Prefecture–designated cultural property), protective enclosure}}]] The main hall was rebuilt in {{jalink|Meiwa|Q1146161}} 5 ({{jalink|1768|Q7675}}) during the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}} by the seventh-generation Sukumo Doi chief retainer {{jalink|Yamauchi Ujitsura|Q11466439}} and his heir Ujimasa. It is a three-bay {{jalink|Nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} structure with a cypress-bark roof. The interior is divided into an inner and outer sanctuary. Some carvings—such as elephant-nose brackets, frog-leg struts, and decorative supports—date from an early Edo reconstruction. The building is designated a Protected Tangible Cultural Property of Kōchi Prefecture and is currently enshrined within a protective enclosure{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}{{Sfn|Cultural Properties of Sukumo City (Sukumo City History)}}. The {{jalink|Ichiigashi|Q3414213}} (Castanopsis cuspidata) in front of the shrine is the largest tree in the shrine grove, measuring 29.0 meters in height and estimated to be 400 years old. It is designated a Natural Monument of Sukumo City{{Sfn|Cultural Properties of Sukumo City (Sukumo City History)}}On-site explanatory sign.. link|Q54153260}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Takachiniimasu Shrine |Image = [[File:Takachiniimasu-jinja, shaden.jpg|280px]]
Main sanctuary |Location = {{jalink|Kōchi Prefecture|Q134093}}{{jalink|Sukumo City|Q655258}} Hirata-chō Tonai |Coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deity = {{jalink|Kotoshironushi|Q8009611|Tomihayayae Kotoshironushi-no-Kami}} |Sacred object = |Shrine rank = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (minor)
Former {{jalink|Gōsha|Q134917316}} |Founded = Unknown |Annual festival = {{jalink|September 22 (old calendar)|Q840122|Old calendar September 22}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}} |Main hall = Three-bay {{jalink|Nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} |Pilgrimage designation = |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Kochi|label=Takachiniimasu Shrine|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[File:Takachiniimasu-jinja, torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Takachiniimasu Shrine''' (たかちにますじんじゃ / たかちにいますじんじゃ, also written Takachiza Shrine) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} located in Hirata-chō Tonai, {{jalink|Sukumo City|Q655258}}, {{jalink|Kōchi Prefecture|Q134093}}. It is a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}, and under the {{jalink|Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|former ranking system}} it was classified as a {{jalink|Gōsha|Q134917316}}. === Enshrined deities === The shrine enshrines the following three deities{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}}. * Principal deity: {{jalink|Kotoshironushi|Q8009611|Tomihayayae Kotoshironushi-no-Kami}} * Right auxiliary shrine: {{jalink|Ōkuninushi|Q276944|Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto}} * Left auxiliary shrine: {{jalink|Susanoo|Q272993|Susanoo-no-Mikoto}} In the ''{{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}}'' {{jalink|Jinmyōchō|Q11064932}} compiled in {{jalink|Engi (era)|Q1327274}} 5 ({{jalink|927|Q30470}}), it is listed simply as “Takachiniimasu Shrine (the shrine of the deity enshrined at Takachi),” without specifying the enshrined deities. The name “{{Ruby|Takachi|たかち}}” is an ancient toponym unrelated to the present-day place name of Kōchi City (which developed during the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}} through the changes Kawauchi → Taka-chi → Kōchi“Kōchi Castle,” ''Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 40: Place Names of Kōchi Prefecture'', Heibonsha, 1983.). In historical sources it also appears as “Takaji” or “Takamochi”{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. Its origin is unclear, but ''Tosa no Kuni Shikishakō'' points out a phonetic correspondence between “Takachi” and “Takaichi” (in {{jalink|Yamato Province|Q907495}} {{jalink|Takaichi District|Q1117901}} there is {{jalink|Kawamata Shrine|Q11553360|Kamo Kotoshironushi Shrine}}){{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. According to ''Tosa no Kuni Shikishakō'', the {{jalink|shintai|Q327532}} of the principal deity is a blue-black jade stone; that of the left auxiliary deity is a blue stone; and that of the right auxiliary deity is a wooden statue{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}}. === History === The date of founding is unknown{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. Nearby stood one of Kōchi Prefecture’s representative tumuli, the {{jalink|Hirata Sogayama Kofun|Q22120134}} (now lost), and a theory has been proposed that this kofun and the shrine together formed the tomb and ritual site of the {{jalink|Hata no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Q21654186}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. The ''{{jalink|Sendai Kuji Hongi|Q2716751}}'' “{{jalink|Kokuzō Hongi|Q133833194}}” records that during the reign of the 10th emperor {{jalink|Emperor Sujin|Q333530}}, Amenokara-sumi-no-Mikoto was appointed Hata no Kuni no Miyatsuko, leading to a theory that the shrine was founded around this time, though details are unclear{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}}. Ritual remains have been identified within the precincts, and artifacts such as {{jalink|Haji ware|Q5639938}}, {{jalink|Sue ware|Q7634351}}, and jars from the {{jalink|Heian period|Q193292}} have been excavated, as well as a chipped stone axe from the late {{jalink|Jōmon period|Q52824}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}}. In the ''{{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}}'' {{jalink|Jinmyōchō|Q11064932}} compiled in {{jalink|Engi (era)|Q1327274}} 5 ({{jalink|927|Q30470}}), the shrine is listed in {{jalink|Tosa Province|Q857330}} {{jalink|Hata District|Q1132241}} as “Takachiniimasu Shrine” and ranked as a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}}. Among the place names appearing in the ''Wamyōshō'', the present location is identified with Hirata-gō (also written Makita-gō in variant texts) of Hata District{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}}. During the {{jalink|medieval period|Q12554}}, there was a repair of the shrine buildings in {{jalink|Genkyū|Q1194276}} 2 ({{jalink|1205|Q5229}}), and Chōfuku-ji served as its {{jalink|bettō-ji|Q11396755}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}}. An inscription on a waniguchi bell dated {{jalink|Ōei|Q1193078}} 6 ({{jalink|1399|Q6422}}) cited in ''Nanroshi'' reads “Takachi no Daimyōjin,” while a ridge tag from {{jalink|Tenbun (era)|Q1192853}} 13 ({{jalink|1544|Q6525}}) reads “Takamochisha Daimyōjin,” recording construction carried out by {{jalink|Ichijō Fusayori|Q11352818}}, the fourth head of the {{jalink|Tosa Ichijō clan|Q11423000}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}}. A land survey register from {{jalink|Tenshō|Q1145058}} 17 ({{jalink|1589|Q6759}}) refers to “Takamochi Shrine” and records the existence of a shrine floor, treasure hall, dance hall, and side hall{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}}. In the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}}, the shrine was venerated by the Yamauchi (Andō) family, chief retainers of the {{jalink|Tosa Domain|Q1189704}} at Sukumo Doi, and in {{jalink|Meiwa|Q1146161}} 5 ({{jalink|1768|Q7675}}) the present main hall was rebuilt by the seventh-generation retainer {{jalink|Yamauchi Ujitsura|Q11466439}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. After the {{jalink|Meiji Restoration|Q8707}}, the shrine was ranked as a {{jalink|Gōsha|Q134917316}} under the {{jalink|Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}}. === Shrine grounds === [[File:Takachiniimasu-jinja, honden.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Main hall (Kōchi Prefecture–designated cultural property), protective enclosure}}]] The main hall was rebuilt in {{jalink|Meiwa|Q1146161}} 5 ({{jalink|1768|Q7675}}) during the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}} by the seventh-generation Sukumo Doi chief retainer {{jalink|Yamauchi Ujitsura|Q11466439}} and his heir Ujimasa. It is a three-bay {{jalink|Nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} structure with a cypress-bark roof. The interior is divided into an inner and outer sanctuary. Some carvings—such as elephant-nose brackets, frog-leg struts, and decorative supports—date from an early Edo reconstruction. The building is designated a Protected Tangible Cultural Property of Kōchi Prefecture and is currently enshrined within a protective enclosure{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}{{Sfn|Cultural Properties of Sukumo City (Sukumo City History)}}. The {{jalink|Ichiigashi|Q3414213}} (Castanopsis cuspidata) in front of the shrine is the largest tree in the shrine grove, measuring 29.0 meters in height and estimated to be 400 years old. It is designated a Natural Monument of Sukumo City{{Sfn|Cultural Properties of Sukumo City (Sukumo City History)}}On-site explanatory sign.. link|Q54153260}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Takachiniimasu Shrine |Image = [[File:Takachiniimasu-jinja, shaden.jpg|280px]]
Main sanctuary |Location = {{ill|lt=Kōchi Prefecture|Q134093|WD=Q134093}}{{ill|lt=Sukumo City|Q655258|WD=Q655258}} Hirata-chō Tonai |Coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deity = {{ill|lt=Kotoshironushi|Q8009611|WD=Q8009611|lt=Tomihayayae Kotoshironushi-no-Kami}} |Sacred object = |Shrine rank = {{ill||lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}} (minor)
Former {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}} |Founded = Unknown |Annual festival = {{ill|lt=September 22 (old calendar)|Q840122|WD=Q840122|lt=Old calendar September 22}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}} |Main hall = Three-bay {{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} |Pilgrimage designation = |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Kochi|label=Takachiniimasu Shrine|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[File:Takachiniimasu-jinja, torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Takachiniimasu Shrine''' (たかちにますじんじゃ / たかちにいますじんじゃ, also written Takachiza Shrine) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} located in Hirata-chō Tonai, {{ill|lt=Sukumo City|Q655258|WD=Q655258}}, {{ill|lt=Kōchi Prefecture|Q134093|WD=Q134093}}. It is a {{ill||lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}}, and under the {{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former ranking system}} it was classified as a {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}}. === Enshrined deities === The shrine enshrines the following three deities{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}}. * Principal deity: {{ill|lt=Kotoshironushi|Q8009611|WD=Q8009611|lt=Tomihayayae Kotoshironushi-no-Kami}} * Right auxiliary shrine: {{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto}} * Left auxiliary shrine: {{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}} In the ''{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448}}'' {{ill|lt=Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}} compiled in {{ill|lt=Engi (era)|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274}} 5 ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470}}), it is listed simply as “Takachiniimasu Shrine (the shrine of the deity enshrined at Takachi),” without specifying the enshrined deities. The name “{{Ruby|Takachi|たかち}}” is an ancient toponym unrelated to the present-day place name of Kōchi City (which developed during the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963}} through the changes Kawauchi → Taka-chi → Kōchi“Kōchi Castle,” ''Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 40: Place Names of Kōchi Prefecture'', Heibonsha, 1983.). In historical sources it also appears as “Takaji” or “Takamochi”{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. Its origin is unclear, but ''Tosa no Kuni Shikishakō'' points out a phonetic correspondence between “Takachi” and “Takaichi” (in {{ill|lt=Yamato Province|Q907495|WD=Q907495}} {{ill|lt=Takaichi District|Q1117901|WD=Q1117901}} there is {{ill|lt=Kawamata Shrine|Q11553360|WD=Q11553360|lt=Kamo Kotoshironushi Shrine}}){{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. According to ''Tosa no Kuni Shikishakō'', the {{ill|lt=shintai|Q327532|WD=Q327532}} of the principal deity is a blue-black jade stone; that of the left auxiliary deity is a blue stone; and that of the right auxiliary deity is a wooden statue{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}}. === History === The date of founding is unknown{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. Nearby stood one of Kōchi Prefecture’s representative tumuli, the {{ill|lt=Hirata Sogayama Kofun|Q22120134|WD=Q22120134}} (now lost), and a theory has been proposed that this kofun and the shrine together formed the tomb and ritual site of the {{ill|lt=Hata no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Q21654186|WD=Q21654186}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. The ''{{ill|lt=Sendai Kuji Hongi|Q2716751|WD=Q2716751}}'' “{{ill|lt=Kokuzō Hongi|Q133833194|WD=Q133833194}}” records that during the reign of the 10th emperor {{ill|lt=Emperor Sujin|Q333530|WD=Q333530}}, Amenokara-sumi-no-Mikoto was appointed Hata no Kuni no Miyatsuko, leading to a theory that the shrine was founded around this time, though details are unclear{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}}. Ritual remains have been identified within the precincts, and artifacts such as {{ill|lt=Haji ware|Q5639938|WD=Q5639938}}, {{ill|lt=Sue ware|Q7634351|WD=Q7634351}}, and jars from the {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292}} have been excavated, as well as a chipped stone axe from the late {{ill|lt=Jōmon period|Q52824|WD=Q52824}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}}. In the ''{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448}}'' {{ill|lt=Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}} compiled in {{ill|lt=Engi (era)|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274}} 5 ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470}}), the shrine is listed in {{ill|lt=Tosa Province|Q857330|WD=Q857330}} {{ill|lt=Hata District|Q1132241|WD=Q1132241}} as “Takachiniimasu Shrine” and ranked as a {{ill||lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}}. Among the place names appearing in the ''Wamyōshō'', the present location is identified with Hirata-gō (also written Makita-gō in variant texts) of Hata District{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}}. During the {{ill|lt=medieval period|Q12554|WD=Q12554}}, there was a repair of the shrine buildings in {{ill|lt=Genkyū|Q1194276|WD=Q1194276}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1205|Q5229|WD=Q5229}}), and Chōfuku-ji served as its {{ill|lt=bettō-ji|Q11396755|WD=Q11396755}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}}. An inscription on a waniguchi bell dated {{ill|lt=Ōei|Q1193078|WD=Q1193078}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1399|Q6422|WD=Q6422}}) cited in ''Nanroshi'' reads “Takachi no Daimyōjin,” while a ridge tag from {{ill|lt=Tenbun (era)|Q1192853|WD=Q1192853}} 13 ({{ill|lt=1544|Q6525|WD=Q6525}}) reads “Takamochisha Daimyōjin,” recording construction carried out by {{ill|lt=Ichijō Fusayori|Q11352818|WD=Q11352818}}, the fourth head of the {{ill|lt=Tosa Ichijō clan|Q11423000|WD=Q11423000}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}}. A land survey register from {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058}} 17 ({{ill|lt=1589|Q6759|WD=Q6759}}) refers to “Takamochi Shrine” and records the existence of a shrine floor, treasure hall, dance hall, and side hall{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}}. In the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963}}, the shrine was venerated by the Yamauchi (Andō) family, chief retainers of the {{ill|lt=Tosa Domain|Q1189704|WD=Q1189704}} at Sukumo Doi, and in {{ill|lt=Meiwa|Q1146161|WD=Q1146161}} 5 ({{ill|lt=1768|Q7675|WD=Q7675}}) the present main hall was rebuilt by the seventh-generation retainer {{ill|lt=Yamauchi Ujitsura|Q11466439|WD=Q11466439}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. After the {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707}}, the shrine was ranked as a {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}} under the {{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}}. === Shrine grounds === [[File:Takachiniimasu-jinja, honden.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Main hall (Kōchi Prefecture–designated cultural property), protective enclosure}}]] The main hall was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Meiwa|Q1146161|WD=Q1146161}} 5 ({{ill|lt=1768|Q7675|WD=Q7675}}) during the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963}} by the seventh-generation Sukumo Doi chief retainer {{ill|lt=Yamauchi Ujitsura|Q11466439|WD=Q11466439}} and his heir Ujimasa. It is a three-bay {{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} structure with a cypress-bark roof. The interior is divided into an inner and outer sanctuary. Some carvings—such as elephant-nose brackets, frog-leg struts, and decorative supports—date from an early Edo reconstruction. The building is designated a Protected Tangible Cultural Property of Kōchi Prefecture and is currently enshrined within a protective enclosure{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}{{Sfn|Cultural Properties of Sukumo City (Sukumo City History)}}. The {{ill|lt=Ichiigashi|Q3414213|WD=Q3414213}} (Castanopsis cuspidata) in front of the shrine is the largest tree in the shrine grove, measuring 29.0 meters in height and estimated to be 400 years old. It is designated a Natural Monument of Sukumo City{{Sfn|Cultural Properties of Sukumo City (Sukumo City History)}}On-site explanatory sign.. File:Takachiniimasu-jinja, ichiigashi.jpg|Ichiigashi (Sukumo City–designated Natural Monument) === Auxiliary and branch shrines === [[File:Takachiniimasu-jinja, keidaisha-2.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Left shrine}}]] [[File:Takachiniimasu-jinja, keidaisha-1.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Right shrine}}]] * Left shrine (enshrined to the left when facing the main hall) ** Hijiri Shrine ** Kōtaishi Shrine ** Soga Shrine ** Tenmangū * Right shrine (enshrined to the right when facing the main hall) ** Shibaori Shrine ** Niida Shrine ** Ōfune Shrine ** Hiryū Shrine ** Taka Shrine === Cultural properties === === Kōchi Prefecture–designated cultural properties === * Protected Tangible Cultural Property ** Main hall (structure) – Edo period; rebuilt in Meiwa 5 (1768). Designated on February 15, 1955 (Shōwa 30)[http://www.kochinet.ed.jp/bunkazai/details/110-2/110-2-07.htm Takachiza Shrine Main Hall] (Kōchi Prefecture website).[http://www.city.sukumo.kochi.jp/sbc/history/cultural/p002.html Takachiniimasu Shrine Main Hall] (Sukumo City website).{{Sfn|Cultural Properties of Sukumo City (Sukumo City History)}}. === Sukumo City–designated cultural properties === * Protected Tangible Cultural Property ** Half-bodied lion statues – Works from the {{ill|lt=Kamakura period|Q236205|WD=Q236205}}. Two Karajishi statues made of {{ill|lt=hinoki|Q917187|WD=Q917187}} wood, each 32.5 cm tall; both lack half of the body. Designated on July 24, 1963 (Shōwa 38){{Sfn|Cultural Properties of Sukumo City (Sukumo City History)}}. * Natural Monument ** Hirata no Ichiigashi – Designated on July 24, 1963 (Shōwa 38){{Sfn|Cultural Properties of Sukumo City (Sukumo City History)}}. === Notes === {{Help:Footnotes}} {{Reflist|2}} === References === * On-site explanatory sign * Publications by local governments ** Main sanctuary |Location = {{ill|lt=Kōchi Prefecture|Q134093|WD=Q134093}}{{ill|lt=Sukumo City|Q655258|WD=Q655258}} Hirata-chō Tonai |Coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deity = {{ill|lt=Kotoshironushi|Q8009611|WD=Q8009611|lt=Tomihayayae Kotoshironushi-no-Kami}} |Sacred object = |Shrine rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}} (minor)
Former {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}} |Founded = Unknown |Annual festival = {{ill|lt=September 22 (old calendar)|Q840122|WD=Q840122|lt=Old calendar September 22}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}} |Main hall = Three-bay {{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} |Pilgrimage designation = |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Kochi|label=Takachiniimasu Shrine|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[File:Takachiniimasu-jinja, torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Takachiniimasu Shrine''' (たかちにますじんじゃ / たかちにいますじんじゃ, also written Takachiza Shrine) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} located in Hirata-chō Tonai, {{ill|lt=Sukumo City|Q655258|WD=Q655258}}, {{ill|lt=Kōchi Prefecture|Q134093|WD=Q134093}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}}, and under the {{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former ranking system}} it was classified as a {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}}. === Enshrined deities === The shrine enshrines the following three deities{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}}. * Principal deity: {{ill|lt=Kotoshironushi|Q8009611|WD=Q8009611|lt=Tomihayayae Kotoshironushi-no-Kami}} * Right auxiliary shrine: {{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto}} * Left auxiliary shrine: {{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}} In the ''{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448}}'' {{ill|lt=Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}} compiled in {{ill|lt=Engi (era)|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274}} 5 ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470}}), it is listed simply as “Takachiniimasu Shrine (the shrine of the deity enshrined at Takachi),” without specifying the enshrined deities. The name “{{Ruby|Takachi|たかち}}” is an ancient toponym unrelated to the present-day place name of Kōchi City (which developed during the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963}} through the changes Kawauchi → Taka-chi → Kōchi“Kōchi Castle,” ''Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 40: Place Names of Kōchi Prefecture'', Heibonsha, 1983.). In historical sources it also appears as “Takaji” or “Takamochi”{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. Its origin is unclear, but ''Tosa no Kuni Shikishakō'' points out a phonetic correspondence between “Takachi” and “Takaichi” (in {{ill|lt=Yamato Province|Q907495|WD=Q907495}} {{ill|lt=Takaichi District|Q1117901|WD=Q1117901}} there is {{ill|lt=Kawamata Shrine|Q11553360|WD=Q11553360|lt=Kamo Kotoshironushi Shrine}}){{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. According to ''Tosa no Kuni Shikishakō'', the {{ill|lt=shintai|Q327532|WD=Q327532}} of the principal deity is a blue-black jade stone; that of the left auxiliary deity is a blue stone; and that of the right auxiliary deity is a wooden statue{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}}. === History === The date of founding is unknown{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. Nearby stood one of Kōchi Prefecture’s representative tumuli, the {{ill|lt=Hirata Sogayama Kofun|Q22120134|WD=Q22120134}} (now lost), and a theory has been proposed that this kofun and the shrine together formed the tomb and ritual site of the {{ill|lt=Hata no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Q21654186|WD=Q21654186}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. The ''{{ill|lt=Sendai Kuji Hongi|Q2716751|WD=Q2716751}}'' “{{ill|lt=Kokuzō Hongi|Q133833194|WD=Q133833194}}” records that during the reign of the 10th emperor {{ill|lt=Emperor Sujin|Q333530|WD=Q333530}}, Amenokara-sumi-no-Mikoto was appointed Hata no Kuni no Miyatsuko, leading to a theory that the shrine was founded around this time, though details are unclear{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}}. Ritual remains have been identified within the precincts, and artifacts such as {{ill|lt=Haji ware|Q5639938|WD=Q5639938}}, {{ill|lt=Sue ware|Q7634351|WD=Q7634351}}, and jars from the {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292}} have been excavated, as well as a chipped stone axe from the late {{ill|lt=Jōmon period|Q52824|WD=Q52824}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}}. In the ''{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448}}'' {{ill|lt=Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}} compiled in {{ill|lt=Engi (era)|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274}} 5 ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470}}), the shrine is listed in {{ill|lt=Tosa Province|Q857330|WD=Q857330}} {{ill|lt=Hata District|Q1132241|WD=Q1132241}} as “Takachiniimasu Shrine” and ranked as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}}. Among the place names appearing in the ''Wamyōshō'', the present location is identified with Hirata-gō (also written Makita-gō in variant texts) of Hata District{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}}. During the {{ill|lt=medieval period|Q12554|WD=Q12554}}, there was a repair of the shrine buildings in {{ill|lt=Genkyū|Q1194276|WD=Q1194276}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1205|Q5229|WD=Q5229}}), and Chōfuku-ji served as its {{ill|lt=bettō-ji|Q11396755|WD=Q11396755}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}}. An inscription on a waniguchi bell dated {{ill|lt=Ōei|Q1193078|WD=Q1193078}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1399|Q6422|WD=Q6422}}) cited in ''Nanroshi'' reads “Takachi no Daimyōjin,” while a ridge tag from {{ill|lt=Tenbun (era)|Q1192853|WD=Q1192853}} 13 ({{ill|lt=1544|Q6525|WD=Q6525}}) reads “Takamochisha Daimyōjin,” recording construction carried out by {{ill|lt=Ichijō Fusayori|Q11352818|WD=Q11352818}}, the fourth head of the {{ill|lt=Tosa Ichijō clan|Q11423000|WD=Q11423000}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}}. A land survey register from {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058}} 17 ({{ill|lt=1589|Q6759|WD=Q6759}}) refers to “Takamochi Shrine” and records the existence of a shrine floor, treasure hall, dance hall, and side hall{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}}. In the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963}}, the shrine was venerated by the Yamauchi (Andō) family, chief retainers of the {{ill|lt=Tosa Domain|Q1189704|WD=Q1189704}} at Sukumo Doi, and in {{ill|lt=Meiwa|Q1146161|WD=Q1146161}} 5 ({{ill|lt=1768|Q7675|WD=Q7675}}) the present main hall was rebuilt by the seventh-generation retainer {{ill|lt=Yamauchi Ujitsura|Q11466439|WD=Q11466439}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. After the {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707}}, the shrine was ranked as a {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}} under the {{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}}. === Shrine grounds === [[File:Takachiniimasu-jinja, honden.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Main hall (Kōchi Prefecture–designated cultural property), protective enclosure}}]] The main hall was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Meiwa|Q1146161|WD=Q1146161}} 5 ({{ill|lt=1768|Q7675|WD=Q7675}}) during the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963}} by the seventh-generation Sukumo Doi chief retainer {{ill|lt=Yamauchi Ujitsura|Q11466439|WD=Q11466439}} and his heir Ujimasa. It is a three-bay {{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} structure with a cypress-bark roof. The interior is divided into an inner and outer sanctuary. Some carvings—such as elephant-nose brackets, frog-leg struts, and decorative supports—date from an early Edo reconstruction. The building is designated a Protected Tangible Cultural Property of Kōchi Prefecture and is currently enshrined within a protective enclosure{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}{{Sfn|Cultural Properties of Sukumo City (Sukumo City History)}}. The {{ill|lt=Ichiigashi|Q3414213|WD=Q3414213}} (Castanopsis cuspidata) in front of the shrine is the largest tree in the shrine grove, measuring 29.0 meters in height and estimated to be 400 years old. It is designated a Natural Monument of Sukumo City{{Sfn|Cultural Properties of Sukumo City (Sukumo City History)}}On-site explanatory sign.. File:Takachiniimasu-jinja, ichiigashi.jpg|Ichiigashi (Sukumo City–designated Natural Monument) === Auxiliary and branch shrines === [[File:Takachiniimasu-jinja, keidaisha-2.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Left shrine}}]] [[File:Takachiniimasu-jinja, keidaisha-1.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Right shrine}}]] * Left shrine (enshrined to the left when facing the main hall) ** Hijiri Shrine ** Kōtaishi Shrine ** Soga Shrine ** Tenmangū * Right shrine (enshrined to the right when facing the main hall) ** Shibaori Shrine ** Niida Shrine ** Ōfune Shrine ** Hiryū Shrine ** Taka Shrine === Cultural properties === === Kōchi Prefecture–designated cultural properties === * Protected Tangible Cultural Property ** Main hall (structure) – Edo period; rebuilt in Meiwa 5 (1768). Designated on February 15, 1955 (Shōwa 30)[http://www.kochinet.ed.jp/bunkazai/details/110-2/110-2-07.htm Takachiza Shrine Main Hall] (Kōchi Prefecture website).[http://www.city.sukumo.kochi.jp/sbc/history/cultural/p002.html Takachiniimasu Shrine Main Hall] (Sukumo City website).{{Sfn|Cultural Properties of Sukumo City (Sukumo City History)}}. === Sukumo City–designated cultural properties === * Protected Tangible Cultural Property ** Half-bodied lion statues – Works from the {{ill|lt=Kamakura period|Q236205|WD=Q236205}}. Two Karajishi statues made of {{ill|lt=hinoki|Q917187|WD=Q917187}} wood, each 32.5 cm tall; both lack half of the body. Designated on July 24, 1963 (Shōwa 38){{Sfn|Cultural Properties of Sukumo City (Sukumo City History)}}. * Natural Monument ** Hirata no Ichiigashi – Designated on July 24, 1963 (Shōwa It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN}}, and under the {{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former ranking system}} it was classified as a {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}}. === Enshrined deities === The shrine enshrines the following three deities{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}}. * Principal deity: {{ill|lt=Kotoshironushi|Q8009611|WD=Q8009611|lt=Tomihayayae Kotoshironushi-no-Kami}} * Right auxiliary shrine: {{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto}} * Left auxiliary shrine: {{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}} In the ''{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448}}'' {{ill|lt=Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}} compiled in {{ill|lt=Engi (era)|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274}} 5 ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470}}), it is listed simply as “Takachiniimasu Shrine (the shrine of the deity enshrined at Takachi),” without specifying the enshrined deities. The name “{{Ruby|Takachi|たかち}}” is an ancient toponym unrelated to the present-day place name of Kōchi City (which developed during the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963}} through the changes Kawauchi → Taka-chi → Kōchi“Kōchi Castle,” ''Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 40: Place Names of Kōchi Prefecture'', Heibonsha, 1983.). In historical sources it also appears as “Takaji” or “Takamochi”{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. Its origin is unclear, but ''Tosa no Kuni Shikishakō'' points out a phonetic correspondence between “Takachi” and “Takaichi” (in {{ill|lt=Yamato Province|Q907495|WD=Q907495}} {{ill|lt=Takaichi District|Q1117901|WD=Q1117901}} there is {{ill|lt=Kawamata Shrine|Q11553360|WD=Q11553360|lt=Kamo Kotoshironushi Shrine}}){{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. According to ''Tosa no Kuni Shikishakō'', the {{ill|lt=shintai|Q327532|WD=Q327532}} of the principal deity is a blue-black jade stone; that of the left auxiliary deity is a blue stone; and that of the right auxiliary deity is a wooden statue{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}}. === History === The date of founding is unknown{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. Nearby stood one of Kōchi Prefecture’s representative tumuli, the {{ill|lt=Hirata Sogayama Kofun|Q22120134|WD=Q22120134}} (now lost), and a theory has been proposed that this kofun and the shrine together formed the tomb and ritual site of the {{ill|lt=Hata no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Q21654186|WD=Q21654186}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. The ''{{ill|lt=Sendai Kuji Hongi|Q2716751|WD=Q2716751}}'' “{{ill|lt=Kokuzō Hongi|Q133833194|WD=Q133833194}}” records that during the reign of the 10th emperor {{ill|lt=Emperor Sujin|Q333530|WD=Q333530}}, Amenokara-sumi-no-Mikoto was appointed Hata no Kuni no Miyatsuko, leading to a theory that the shrine was founded around this time, though details are unclear{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}}. Ritual remains have been identified within the precincts, and artifacts such as {{ill|lt=Haji ware|Q5639938|WD=Q5639938}}, {{ill|lt=Sue ware|Q7634351|WD=Q7634351}}, and jars from the {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292}} have been excavated, as well as a chipped stone axe from the late {{ill|lt=Jōmon period|Q52824|WD=Q52824}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}}. In the ''{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448}}'' {{ill|lt=Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}} compiled in {{ill|lt=Engi (era)|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274}} 5 ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470}}), the shrine is listed in {{ill|lt=Tosa Province|Q857330|WD=Q857330}} {{ill|lt=Hata District|Q1132241|WD=Q1132241}} as “Takachiniimasu Shrine” and ranked as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}}. Among the place names appearing in the ''Wamyōshō'', the present location is identified with Hirata-gō (also written Makita-gō in variant texts) of Hata District{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}}. During the {{ill|lt=medieval period|Q12554|WD=Q12554}}, there was a repair of the shrine buildings in {{ill|lt=Genkyū|Q1194276|WD=Q1194276}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1205|Q5229|WD=Q5229}}), and Chōfuku-ji served as its {{ill|lt=bettō-ji|Q11396755|WD=Q11396755}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}}. An inscription on a waniguchi bell dated {{ill|lt=Ōei|Q1193078|WD=Q1193078}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1399|Q6422|WD=Q6422}}) cited in ''Nanroshi'' reads “Takachi no Daimyōjin,” while a ridge tag from {{ill|lt=Tenbun (era)|Q1192853|WD=Q1192853}} 13 ({{ill|lt=1544|Q6525|WD=Q6525}}) reads “Takamochisha Daimyōjin,” recording construction carried out by {{ill|lt=Ichijō Fusayori|Q11352818|WD=Q11352818}}, the fourth head of the {{ill|lt=Tosa Ichijō clan|Q11423000|WD=Q11423000}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}}. A land survey register from {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058}} 17 ({{ill|lt=1589|Q6759|WD=Q6759}}) refers to “Takamochi Shrine” and records the existence of a shrine floor, treasure hall, dance hall, and side hall{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}}. In the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963}}, the shrine was venerated by the Yamauchi (Andō) family, chief retainers of the {{ill|lt=Tosa Domain|Q1189704|WD=Q1189704}} at Sukumo Doi, and in {{ill|lt=Meiwa|Q1146161|WD=Q1146161}} 5 ({{ill|lt=1768|Q7675|WD=Q7675}}) the present main hall was rebuilt by the seventh-generation retainer {{ill|lt=Yamauchi Ujitsura|Q11466439|WD=Q11466439}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. After the {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707}}, the shrine was ranked as a {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}} under the {{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}}. === Shrine grounds === [[File:Takachiniimasu-jinja, honden.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Main hall (Kōchi Prefecture–designated cultural property), protective enclosure}}]] The main hall was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Meiwa|Q1146161|WD=Q1146161}} 5 ({{ill|lt=1768|Q7675|WD=Q7675}}) during the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963}} by the seventh-generation Sukumo Doi chief retainer {{ill|lt=Yamauchi Ujitsura|Q11466439|WD=Q11466439}} and his heir Ujimasa. It is a three-bay {{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} structure with a cypress-bark roof. The interior is divided into an inner and outer sanctuary. Some carvings—such as elephant-nose brackets, frog-leg struts, and decorative supports—date from an early Edo reconstruction. The building is designated a Protected Tangible Cultural Property of Kōchi Prefecture and is currently enshrined within a protective enclosure{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}{{Sfn|Cultural Properties of Sukumo City (Sukumo City History)}}. The {{ill|lt=Ichiigashi|Q3414213|WD=Q3414213}} (Castanopsis cuspidata) in front of the shrine is the largest tree in the shrine grove, measuring 29.0 meters in height and estimated to be 400 years old. It is designated a Natural Monument of Sukumo City{{Sfn|Cultural Properties of Sukumo City (Sukumo City History)}}On-site explanatory sign.. File:Takachiniimasu-jinja, ichiigashi.jpg|Ichiigashi (Sukumo City–designated Natural Monument) === Auxiliary and branch shrines === [[File:Takachiniimasu-jinja, keidaisha-2.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Left shrine}}]] [[File:Takachiniimasu-jinja, keidaisha-1.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Right shrine}}]] * Left shrine (enshrined to the left when facing the main hall) ** Hijiri Shrine ** Kōtaishi Shrine ** Soga Shrine ** Tenmangū * Right shrine (enshrined to the right when facing the main hall) ** Shibaori Shrine ** Niida Shrine ** Ōfune Shrine ** Hiryū Shrine ** Taka Shrine === Cultural properties === === Kōchi Prefecture–designated cultural properties === * Protected Tangible Cultural Property ** Main hall (structure) – Edo period; rebuilt in Meiwa 5 (1768). Designated on February 15, 1955 (Shōwa 30)[http://www.kochinet.ed.jp/bunkazai/details/110-2/110-2-07.htm Takachiza Shrine Main Hall] (Kōchi Prefecture website).[http://www.city.sukumo.kochi.jp/sbc/history/cultural/p002.html Takachiniimasu Shrine Main Hall] (Sukumo City website).{{Sfn|Cultural Properties of Sukumo City (Sukumo City History)}}. === Sukumo City–designated cultural properties === * Protected Tangible Cultural Property ** Half-bodied lion statues – Works from the {{ill|lt=Kamakura period|Q236205|WD=Q236205}}. Two Karajishi statues made of {{ill|lt=hinoki|Q917187|WD=Q917187}} wood, each 32.5 cm tall; both lack half of the body. Designated on July 24, 1963 (Shōwa 38){{Sfn|Cultural Properties of Sukumo City (Sukumo City History)}}. * Natural Monument ** Hirata no Ichiigashi – Designated on July 24, 1963 (Shōwa Nearby stood one of Kōchi Prefecture’s representative tumuli, the {{ill|lt=Hirata Sogayama Kofun|Q22120134|WD=Q22120134}} (now lost), and a theory has been proposed that this kofun and the shrine together formed the tomb and ritual site of the {{ill|lt=Hata no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Q21654186|WD=Q21654186}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. The ''{{ill|lt=Sendai Kuji Hongi|Q2716751|WD=Q2716751}}'' “{{ill|lt=Kokuzō Hongi|Q133833194|WD=Q133833194}}” records that during the reign of the 10th emperor {{ill|lt=Emperor Sujin|Q333530|WD=Q333530}}, Amenokara-sumi-no-Mikoto was appointed Hata no Kuni no Miyatsuko, leading to a theory that the shrine was founded around this time, though details are unclear{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}}. Ritual remains have been identified within the precincts, and artifacts such as {{ill|lt=Haji ware|Q5639938|WD=Q5639938}}, {{ill|lt=Sue ware|Q7634351|WD=Q7634351}}, and jars from the {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292}} have been excavated, as well as a chipped stone axe from the late {{ill|lt=Jōmon period|Q52824|WD=Q52824}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}}. In the ''{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448}}'' {{ill|lt=Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}} compiled in {{ill|lt=Engi (era)|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274}} 5 ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470}}), the shrine is listed in {{ill|lt=Tosa Province|Q857330|WD=Q857330}} {{ill|lt=Hata District|Q1132241|WD=Q1132241}} as “Takachiniimasu Shrine” and ranked as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}}. Among the place names appearing in the ''Wamyōshō'', the present location is identified with Hirata-gō (also written Makita-gō in variant texts) of Hata District{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}}. During the {{ill|lt=medieval period|Q12554|WD=Q12554}}, there was a repair of the shrine buildings in {{ill|lt=Genkyū|Q1194276|WD=Q1194276}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1205|Q5229|WD=Q5229}}), and Chōfuku-ji served as its {{ill|lt=bettō-ji|Q11396755|WD=Q11396755}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}}. An inscription on a waniguchi bell dated {{ill|lt=Ōei|Q1193078|WD=Q1193078}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1399|Q6422|WD=Q6422}}) cited in ''Nanroshi'' reads “Takachi no Daimyōjin,” while a ridge tag from {{ill|lt=Tenbun (era)|Q1192853|WD=Q1192853}} 13 ({{ill|lt=1544|Q6525|WD=Q6525}}) reads “Takamochisha Daimyōjin,” recording construction carried out by {{ill|lt=Ichijō Fusayori|Q11352818|WD=Q11352818}}, the fourth head of the {{ill|lt=Tosa Ichijō clan|Q11423000|WD=Q11423000}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}}. A land survey register from {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058}} 17 ({{ill|lt=1589|Q6759|WD=Q6759}}) refers to “Takamochi Shrine” and records the existence of a shrine floor, treasure hall, dance hall, and side hall{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}}. In the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963}}, the shrine was venerated by the Yamauchi (Andō) family, chief retainers of the {{ill|lt=Tosa Domain|Q1189704|WD=Q1189704}} at Sukumo Doi, and in {{ill|lt=Meiwa|Q1146161|WD=Q1146161}} 5 ({{ill|lt=1768|Q7675|WD=Q7675}}) the present main hall was rebuilt by the seventh-generation retainer {{ill|lt=Yamauchi Ujitsura|Q11466439|WD=Q11466439}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. After the {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707}}, the shrine was ranked as a {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}} under the {{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1987}}. === Shrine grounds === [[File:Takachiniimasu-jinja, honden.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Main hall (Kōchi Prefecture–designated cultural property), protective enclosure}}]] The main hall was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Meiwa|Q1146161|WD=Q1146161}} 5 ({{ill|lt=1768|Q7675|WD=Q7675}}) during the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963}} by the seventh-generation Sukumo Doi chief retainer {{ill|lt=Yamauchi Ujitsura|Q11466439|WD=Q11466439}} and his heir Ujimasa. It is a three-bay {{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} structure with a cypress-bark roof. The interior is divided into an inner and outer sanctuary. Some carvings—such as elephant-nose brackets, frog-leg struts, and decorative supports—date from an early Edo reconstruction. The building is designated a Protected Tangible Cultural Property of Kōchi Prefecture and is currently enshrined within a protective enclosure{{Sfn|Takachiniimasu Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}{{Sfn|Cultural Properties of Sukumo City (Sukumo City History)}}. The {{ill|lt=Ichiigashi|Q3414213|WD=Q3414213}} (Castanopsis cuspidata) in front of the shrine is the largest tree in the shrine grove, measuring 29.0 meters in height and estimated to be 400 years old. It is designated a Natural Monument of Sukumo City{{Sfn|Cultural Properties of Sukumo City (Sukumo City History)}}On-site explanatory sign.. Designated on February 15, 1955 (Shōwa 30)[http://www.kochinet.ed.jp/bunkazai/details/110-2/110-2-07.htm Takachiza Shrine Main Hall] (Kōchi Prefecture website).[http://www.city.sukumo.kochi.jp/sbc/history/cultural/p002.html Takachiniimasu Shrine Main Hall] (Sukumo City website).{{Sfn|Cultural Properties of Sukumo City (Sukumo City History)}}. === Sukumo City–designated cultural properties === * Protected Tangible Cultural Property ** Half-bodied lion statues – Works from the {{ill|lt=Kamakura period|Q236205|WD=Q236205}}.
